title: Meta ships Muse Image, Superintelligence Labs' first shipped image model
subtitle: The free multimodal generator lands across Meta AI, Instagram, and WhatsApp and ranks second only to GPT Image 2 on public text-to-image and image-editing benchmarks.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: On July 7, 2026, Meta unveiled Muse Image, a new image-generation and editing model built by Meta Superintelligence Labs. The model — internally code-named Mango — is available free through the Meta AI app, on Instagram Stories, and in WhatsApp. It supports text-to-image generation, single-image editing, multi-image composition, and instruction-based edits driven by sketches or annotations drawn directly on a photo. A subscription tier gates heavy usage, and Meta says a broader rollout to Facebook and Messenger will follow in the coming weeks.

Muse Image ranks second on the leading public benchmarks for text-to-image, single-image editing, and multi-image editing — behind OpenAI's GPT Image 2 in each. That is a strong debut relative to Meta's prior image work, which had trailed Adobe Firefly, Midjourney, and OpenAI's image models for two years. The features Meta is pushing hardest — one-tap photo restoration, identity transfers that render you as a claymation character or a 16-bit sprite, and functional QR-code generation from natural-language prompts — are the parts of the product built for social sharing, not workflow.

The launch is Meta's clearest signal yet that Superintelligence Labs, the unit consolidated under Alexandr Wang in mid-2025, is shipping product and not only training runs. It also concentrates a distribution advantage that neither OpenAI nor Anthropic can match: Muse Image is inside apps that reach roughly 3.9 billion monthly users. Users have already raised objections about photos being ingested for personalization and training, and Meta's opt-out flow — buried under Meta AI settings — is the piece of the launch most worth watching over the next few weeks.

Takeaway for learners: the frontier of image generation has moved from 'can it draw a cat' to instruction-based editing and identity-preserving transformation. The prompting skill that separates hobby use from production work is now describing what to change without touching what to keep. Practice iterative editing prompts rather than one-shot generations if you want the work to hold up in publishing, marketing, or journalism contexts.
